title: With the companion protocol, I can see

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: There is no public key associated with channel messages, so it's not possible. Only a sender name is attached
messages.content_html: There is no public key associated with channel messages, so it's not possible. Only a sender name is attached
messages.reply_html:
Daniel
@Liam Cottle Hey do you perhaps have some pointers here in terms of how the app is getting the contact names for private and especially channel messages? The private messages seem to have a pubkey prefix and while not the full pub key it's at least workable. The channel messages do not have any indication in terms of who sent it. How is it possible to get a unique id for who has sent a private or channel message?

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: Ow, that means channel messages can get spoofed and there is no way to figure out a verified/trusted sender. Meaning I can rename myself to any (potentially existing) name I want to spoof and send a message out. How is the mobile app doing this, simply parsing everything before the first ":" in the channel message text? Regarding the 6-byte prefix in private messages: I'm more concerned about being able to spoof / forge it, as it's realistic to brute force a 6-byte key prefix. Are these two problems something an iteration of the protocol can and will address? Because as is right now this sounds like quite the attack vectors especially with regards to channel messages.
messages.content_html: Ow, that means channel messages can get spoofed and there is no way to figure out a verified/trusted sender. Meaning I can rename myself to any (potentially existing) name I want to spoof and send a message out. How is the mobile app doing this, simply parsing everything before the first ":" in the channel message text? Regarding the 6-byte prefix in private messages: I'm more concerned about being able to spoof / forge it, as it's realistic to brute force a 6-byte key prefix. Are these two problems something an iteration of the protocol can and will address? Because as is right now this sounds like quite the attack vectors especially with regards to channel messages.

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: Can't spoof DMs, they're encrypted with a shared secret only know to the sender/recipient
messages.content_html: Can't spoof DMs, they're encrypted with a shared secret only know to the sender/recipient

Daniel

messages.author: Daniel
messages.text: but for channel messages there is no authentication wrt. the sender and its identity, yes?
messages.content_html: but for channel messages there is no authentication wrt. the sender and its identity, yes?

messages.author: Liam Cottle
messages.text: Correct, channel messages can have any sender name and are not tied to public keys at all
messages.content_html: Correct, channel messages can have any sender name and are not tied to public keys at all
messages.reply_html:
Daniel
but for channel messages there is no authentication wrt. the sender and its identity, yes?
